section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The European Composer and Songwriter Alliance (ECSA) represents over 30,000 professional composers and songwriters in 28 European countries. With 57 member organisations across Europe, the Alliance speaks for the interests of music creators of art and classical music (contemporary), film and audiovisual music, and popular music. Our mission is to defend and promote the rights of composers and songwriters with the aim of improving their social and economic conditions, as well as their artistic freedom.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: lack of transparency with regards to personalisation and recommendation practices deployed by music streaming platforms and other online platforms.

paragraphs.text: ECSA welcomes the opportunity to provide input on the future Digital Fairness Act and the Commission's focus on addressing remaining shortcomings in the EU's digital rulebook by targeting the lack of digital fairness for consumers, including addressing unfair personalisation practices. As an organisation representing music creators, we are deeply concerned about the pervasive lack of transparency with regards to personalisation and recommendation practices deployed by music streaming platforms and other online platforms.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: Recommender systems constitute the base of personalisation practices in music streaming, exercising great influence on the ways in which consumers listen to and discover music.

paragraphs.text: Recommender systems constitute the base of personalisation practices in music streaming, exercising great influence on the ways in which consumers listen to and discover music. These recommender systems mostly consist of (AI-powered) algorithmic recommendations and algorithmic playlists. Because of their power in shaping consumers' choices, they can have a great impact on the livelihoods of the music creators we represent, determining to whom and when their music will be recommended and thus influencing the success of their works and the revenue they generate. More broadly, recommender systems and personalisation practices can ultimately shape our societies' cultural identity and determine which voices and experiences are amplified.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: The opaque and proprietary algorithms used by streaming platforms can also reproduce and amplify biases against certain creators, genres and works in ways that are unfair.
Among various issues, there is evidence that these algorithms tend to suffer from several biases, for example by favouring items that are already the most popular (the "popularity bias").

paragraphs.text: The opaque and proprietary algorithms used by streaming platforms can also reproduce and amplify biases against certain creators, genres and works in ways that are unfair. Among various issues, there is evidence that these algorithms tend to suffer from several biases, for example by favouring items that are already the most popular (the "popularity bias"). Moreover, the high level of concentration in the music sector (with only three major record labels) is also a matter of concern since playlists are often prioritising the three major labels' repertoires at the expense of more culturally

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: diverse music. Although the full impact and fairness of these recommender systems and their functioning is difficult to assess due to their opacity and the lack of available data, researchers have associated the music streaming model with issues such as the emergence of a "superstar economy", a lack of song turnover and reduced acoustic diversity.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: These factors point to the clear need to address the disproportionate and unfair impact that opaque recommender systems, algorithmic recommendations and playlisting have on cultural and linguistic diversity, the power of consumers of making well-informed decisions, and the livelihoods of the music creators ECSA represents.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: We strongly believe that the Commission ought to duly take these considerations into account in the context of the Digital Fairness Act, and propose the following measures:

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Introduce transparency obligations for music streaming platforms to disclose information about the functioning of algorithmic recommendations and playlists

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers should have the right to be informed about how content is recommended to them (knowing which factors are taken into account, and what kind of personal data is used).
opt out of personalisation practices that are based on their personal data.

paragraphs.text: This obligation would serve a double function. First, consumers should have the right to be informed about how content is recommended to them (knowing which factors are taken into account, and what kind of personal data is used). Second, being able to accurately assess how recommender systems operate can inform policy-makers about the impact of these systems on cultural and linguistic diversity, the respect and promotion of which are among the objectives of the EU. While the EU adopted legislative measures to promote cultural diversity and European works in the audiovisual sector with the AVMS Directive, it lacks similar instruments for the music sector and music streaming services. Transparency requirements also form a key milestone of the provisions on the Directive 2019/790 on Copyright in the Digital Single Market (Art. 19). Without transparency, composers and songwriters and/or the collective management organisations managing their rights are not able to assess the success of their works and the level of remuneration due to them by their contractual counterparts. The introduction of a transparency obligation for music recommender systems would thus also contribute to the effective implementation and enforcement of the objectives of the CDSM Directive's transparency provisions. Furthermore, this transparency obligation could also be complemented with an obligation to provide consumers the possibility to opt out of personalisation practices that are based on their personal data.

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: Introduce an obligation for streaming platforms to clearly label AI-generated content

paragraphs.text: Introduce an obligation for streaming platforms to clearly label AI-generated content

section_path: ECSA's response to a call for evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: AI-generated music is able to gain millions of streams while deceiving consumers about what they are listening to.

paragraphs.text: In September, music streaming service Deezer reported that 30,000 AI-generated tracks are uploaded to the platform daily (10% more than reported in April). Furthermore, cases of AI “artists” such as that of AI band Velvet Sundown indicate that AI-generated music is able to gain millions of streams while deceiving consumers about what they are listening to. Coupled with the opacity of recommender systems, the rapid rise and diffusion of unlabelled AI-generated music on streaming platforms poses serious questions about transparency for consumers, its impact on cultural and linguistic diversity, and its consequences on the livelihoods of the music creators we represent. These concerns were also brought forward in a recent European Parliament resolution on cultural diversity and the conditions of authors in music streaming (2023/2054(INI)), which stressed the need to ensure that consumers are well-informed about AI-generated works in music streaming. Moreover, the provisions introduced by the AI Act’s Article 50 on AI outputs will not apply to music streaming platforms, as they only target AI developers and deployers, leaving both consumers and creators at risk.
